verses.text: Then Joshua called the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, and said unto them: Ye have kept all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you, and have obeyed my voice in all that I commanded you: ye have not left your brethren these many days unto this day, but have kept the charge of the commandment of the LORD your God. Now the LORD your God hath given rest unto your brethren, as he promised them: therefore now return ye, and get you unto your tents, and unto the land of your possession. But take diligent heed to do the commandment and the law: to love the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, to keep his commandments, to cleave unto him, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul. So Joshua blessed them, and sent them away. Guidance: Seven years of war and the east tribes never went home. They left wives and children behind and crossed the Jordan armed (Num 32, Josh 1, Josh 4). Now Joshua sends them back with a commendation: 'ye have not left your brethren.' And before they go, one final sermon: love the LORD, walk in His ways, keep His commands, cleave, serve with all heart and soul. The discharge papers are written in Deuteronomy 6 language. Going home is the most dangerous mission of all; the only safeguard is staying close to the LORD.

Moses had given to the one half of Manasseh possession in Bashan: but to the other half thereof gave Joshua among their brethren on this side Jordan westward. When Joshua sent them away also unto their tents, then he blessed them, and spake unto them, saying: Return with much riches unto your tents, and with very much cattle, with silver, gold, brass, iron, and very much raiment: divide the spoil of your enemies with your brethren. The children of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh returned, and departed from Shiloh which is in the land of Canaan, to go unto the country of Gilead, to the land of their possession, whereof they were possessed, according to the word of the LORD by the hand of Moses. Guidance: They go home rich — cattle, silver, gold, brass, iron, raiment. And Joshua's last command is generosity: 'divide the spoil with your brethren.' Inheritance is not hoarded; spoil is to be shared with the women and children who held the home front for seven years. The men who fought get spoil; the families who waited get a portion. The economy of the LORD does not forget the people who did not carry the sword.

When they came unto the borders of Jordan, that are in the land of Canaan, the children of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh built there an altar by Jordan — a great altar to see to. The children of Israel heard say, Behold, the children of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh have built an altar over against the land of Canaan, in the borders of Jordan, at the passage of the children of Israel. When the children of Israel heard of it, the whole congregation of the children of Israel gathered themselves together at Shiloh, to go up to war against them. Guidance: Within sight of going home, the east tribes stop and build. A great altar — visible, deliberate, on the Canaan side of the river. The west tribes get the news and instantly muster for war. The book that began with stones of remembrance now closes with stones that nearly start a civil war. The deepest wars in scripture are not against Canaanites but against brothers. Israel's instinct is the right one in this case: better a civil war than a tolerated idolatry.

The children of Israel sent unto the children of Reuben, and Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh, into the land of Gilead, Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest, and with him ten princes — of each chief house a prince throughout all the tribes of Israel, each one a head of the house of their fathers among the thousands of Israel. They came unto the children of Reuben, Gad, and the half tribe of Manasseh, unto the land of Gilead, and spake with them: Guidance: Before swinging swords, Israel sends a delegation. Phinehas — the priest who once stopped a plague at Baal-peor (Num 25) — leads ten princes, one per western tribe. A theological-judicial team. Talk first, war later. This is exactly what civil disputes about doctrine and conscience should look like before they harden into bloodshed: a high-trust, high-authority embassy that asks before it accuses.

Thus saith the whole congregation of the LORD, What trespass is this that ye have committed against the God of Israel, to turn away this day from following the LORD, in that ye have builded you an altar, that ye might rebel this day against the LORD? Is the iniquity of Peor too little for us, from which we are not cleansed until this day, although there was a plague in the congregation of the LORD, but that ye must turn away this day from following the LORD? It will be, seeing ye rebel today against the LORD, that to morrow he will be wroth with the whole congregation of Israel. Notwithstanding, if the land of your possession be unclean, then pass ye over unto the land of the possession of the LORD, wherein the LORD's tabernacle dwelleth, and take possession among us: but rebel not against the LORD, nor rebel against us, in building you an altar beside the altar of the LORD our God. Did not Achan son of Zerah commit a trespass in the accursed thing, and wrath fell on all the congregation of Israel? and that man perished not alone in his iniquity. Guidance: Two examples are named: Peor (Num 25 — sexual idolatry that killed 24,000) and Achan (Josh 7 — one man's theft killed 36). One sin in the camp touches the whole camp. Phinehas knows this from personal history — he ran a spear through Zimri at Peor. The west tribes do not soft-pedal; they offer the east tribes a stunning solution: if your land is unclean, come live with us. Better to share than to set up rival worship. Real love sometimes calls real questions; truth-telling can offer relocation too.

The children of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh answered, and said unto the heads of the thousands of Israel: The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know: if it be in rebellion, or if in transgression against the LORD (save us not this day), that we have built us an altar to turn from following the LORD, or if to offer thereon burnt offering or meat offering, or if to offer peace offerings thereon, let the LORD himself require it. If we have not rather done it for fear of this thing, saying, In time to come your children might speak unto our children, saying, What have ye to do with the LORD God of Israel? For the LORD hath made Jordan a border between us and you, ye children of Reuben and Gad; ye have no part in the LORD: so shall your children make our children cease from fearing the LORD. Therefore we said, Let us now prepare to build us an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice: but that it may be a witness between us, and you, and our generations after us, that we might do the service of the LORD before him with our burnt offerings, sacrifices, and peace offerings; that your children may not say to our children in time to come, Ye have no part in the LORD. Therefore said we, that it shall be, when they should so say to us or to our generations in time to come, that we may say again, Behold the pattern of the altar of the LORD, which our fathers made, not for burnt offerings, nor for sacrifices; but it is a witness between us and you. God forbid that we should rebel against the LORD, and turn this day from following the LORD, to build an altar for burnt offerings, for meat offerings, or for sacrifices, beside the altar of the LORD our God that is before his tabernacle. Guidance: Three-fold opening — 'The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know.' Maximum oath. Then the explanation: they were afraid. Afraid that the river would become a wall. Afraid that some future generation of west tribes would tell some future generation of east tribes 'you have no part in the LORD.' So they built a replica altar — not for sacrifice (the only altar for that is at Shiloh), but for memory. A theological monument. The east tribes feared accidental exclusion more than misunderstood inclusion. They built a fence around grace.

The east tribes feared accidental exclusion more than misunderstood inclusion. They built a fence around grace.

The children of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh answered, and said unto the heads of the thousands of Israel: The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know: if it be in rebellion, or if in transgression against the LORD (save us not this day), that we have built us an altar to turn from following the LORD, or if to offer thereon burnt offering or meat offering, or if to offer peace offerings thereon, let the LORD himself require it. If we have not rather done it for fear of this thing, saying, In time to come your children might speak unto our children, saying, What have ye to do with the LORD God of Israel? For the LORD hath made Jordan a border between us and you, ye children of Reuben and Gad; ye have no part in the LORD: so shall your children make our children cease from fearing the LORD. Therefore we said, Let us now prepare to build us an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice: but that it may be a witness between us, and you, and our generations after us, that we might do the service of the LORD before him with our burnt offerings, sacrifices, and peace offerings; that your children may not say to our children in time to come, Ye have no part in the LORD. Therefore said we, that it shall be, when they should so say to us or to our generations in time to come, that we may say again, Behold the pattern of the altar of the LORD, which our fathers made, not for burnt offerings, nor for sacrifices; but it is a witness between us and you. God forbid that we should rebel against the LORD, and turn this day from following the LORD, to build an altar for burnt offerings, for meat offerings, or for sacrifices, beside the altar of the LORD our God that is before his tabernacle. Guidance: Three-fold opening — 'The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know.' Maximum oath. Then the explanation: they were afraid. Afraid that the river would become a wall. Afraid that some future generation of west tribes would tell some future generation of east tribes 'you have no part in the LORD.' So they built a replica altar — not for sacrifice (the only altar for that is at Shiloh), but for memory. A theological monument. The east tribes feared accidental exclusion more than misunderstood inclusion. They built a fence around grace.

Afraid that the river would become a wall. Afraid that some future generation of west tribes would tell some future generation of east tribes 'you have no part in the LORD.' The east tribes feared accidental exclusion more than misunderstood inclusion. They built a fence around grace.

When Phinehas the priest, and the princes of the congregation and heads of the thousands of Israel which were with him, heard the words that the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the children of Manasseh spake, it pleased them. Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest said unto the children of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh: This day we perceive that the LORD is among us, because ye have not committed this trespass against the LORD: now ye have delivered the children of Israel out of the hand of the LORD. Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest and the princes returned from the children of Reuben and from the children of Gad, out of the land of Gilead, unto the land of Canaan, to the children of Israel, and brought them word again. Guidance: The mark of a good delegation: when the evidence overturns the accusation, they say so out loud and immediately. 'It pleased them.' Phinehas pivots in one verse from 'rebel against the LORD' to 'the LORD is among us.' He even goes further: 'now ye have delivered the children of Israel out of the hand of the LORD.' The east tribes were not the danger; misunderstanding was. Brothers reconciled is itself rescue from the LORD's anger.

Guidance: The mark of a good delegation: when the evidence overturns the accusation, they say so out loud and immediately. 'It pleased them.' Phinehas pivots in one verse from 'rebel against the LORD' to 'the LORD is among us.' He even goes further: 'now ye have delivered the children of Israel out of the hand of the LORD.' The east tribes were not the danger; misunderstanding was. Brothers reconciled is itself rescue from the LORD's anger.

When Phinehas the priest, and the princes of the congregation and heads of the thousands of Israel which were with him, heard the words that the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the children of Manasseh spake, it pleased them. Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest said unto the children of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh: This day we perceive that the LORD is among us, because ye have not committed this trespass against the LORD: now ye have delivered the children of Israel out of the hand of the LORD. Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest and the princes returned from the children of Reuben and from the children of Gad, out of the land of Gilead, unto the land of Canaan, to the children of Israel, and brought them word again. Guidance: The mark of a good delegation: when the evidence overturns the accusation, they say so out loud and immediately. 'It pleased them.' Phinehas pivots in one verse from 'rebel against the LORD' to 'the LORD is among us.' He even goes further: 'now ye have delivered the children of Israel out of the hand of the LORD.' The east tribes were not the danger; misunderstanding was. Brothers reconciled is itself rescue from the LORD's anger.

The thing pleased the children of Israel; the children of Israel blessed God, and did not intend to go up against them in battle, to destroy the land wherein the children of Reuben and Gad dwelt. The children of Reuben and the children of Gad called the altar Ed: For it shall be a witness between us that the LORD is God. Guidance: 'Ed' — Hebrew for 'witness.' The same root as 'edah' (assembly, congregation). The altar that almost caused war is given a one-word name about peace: Witness. Its job is to talk for them across the Jordan to their children. Forty years later, when grandchildren ask, 'What is this?' — the stones will answer, 'The LORD is God on both sides of the river.' The book of Joshua, which began with 'be strong and of a good courage,' ends one chapter shy of its close with a different lesson: ask before you charge, listen before you act, and when in doubt, name the altar 'Witness' and let it speak for the next thousand years.
